rows.title: ADR-001: Adopt Next.js for storefront
rows.context: Acme storefront requires SSR for SEO, image optimization, and edge-rendered product pages. The legacy PHP templating engine cannot deliver Core Web Vitals at the 75th percentile required by our 99.95% uptime SLO.
rows.decision: We will build the Acme storefront using Next.js 15 with the App Router, deployed as a CloudFront-fronted Lambda@Edge layer. Server Components handle product catalog rendering; Client Components handle cart and checkout interaction.

positive: Improved LCP from 3.2s to 0.9s on product pages
Unified TS codebase shared with internal admin tools
Edge-rendered ISR for inventory + price changes
negative: Added operational complexity of Lambda@Edge cold starts
Team must learn React Server Components model

alternatives.name: Remix
alternatives.reason_rejected: Smaller ecosystem; weaker image optimization at scale

alternatives.name: Astro
alternatives.reason_rejected: Weak story for cart/checkout interactive flows

rows.title: ADR-002: Envoy as API gateway
rows.context: We need a unified ingress that handles auth, rate limiting, and request transformation across 12 microservices. Per-service ingress (ALB per service) would duplicate auth code 12 times.
rows.decision: We will deploy Envoy as a single-cluster ingress gateway with custom filters for JWT validation and rate limiting. The gateway terminates TLS and routes to internal services via mTLS over a service mesh (Istio).

negative: SRE must operate Envoy/Istio control plane
Latency floor added: ~3ms p50

alternatives.name: AWS API Gateway
alternatives.reason_rejected: Vendor lock-in; per-request pricing prohibitive at 2M MAU

alternatives.name: Kong
alternatives.reason_rejected: Additional control plane to operate alongside Istio

rows.title: ADR-003: Go for cart + shipping services
rows.context: Cart abandonment rate is currently 71%. Latency during the add-to-cart and shipping-estimate flows is a primary driver. We need a runtime that delivers consistent sub-50ms p95 latency at peak (4x normal load on Black Friday).
rows.decision: We will rewrite acme-cart and acme-shipping in Go using Gin and a connection-pooled Postgres client. The existing Node.js implementation will be retained for cart/wishlist read paths during the dual-write migration window.

positive: p95 latency budget drops from 180ms to 35ms
Lower memory footprint per pod (40MB vs 180MB)
negative: Dual-write window introduces data divergence risk
Team must learn Go concurrency primitives

alternatives.name: Rust + axum
alternatives.reason_rejected: Smaller team familiarity; longer development cycle

alternatives.name: Optimize Node.js
alternatives.reason_rejected: Single-threaded model is a hard ceiling for our growth curve

rows.title: ADR-004: Node.js for checkout flow
rows.context: Checkout requires deep integration with payment SDKs, address validation services, and cart state synchronization. The team has 6 years of Node.js production experience.
rows.decision: acme-checkout remains on Node.js (TypeScript, Express). We will use Fastify for new endpoints and migrate incrementally. Server-Sent Events stream order status to the frontend during the 3DS confirmation step.

negative: Continued operational overhead of dual framework
consequences.coordination_notes: ADR-003 proposes Go for write paths in cart, while checkout remains on Node — coordinate dual-write carefully

alternatives.name: Migrate to Go
alternatives.reason_rejected: Payment SDK ecosystem is JS-first; rewrite cost > benefit

rows.title: ADR-005: Java + Spring Boot for payment service
rows.context: PCI-DSS scope minimization requires the payment service to be isolated from the rest of the platform. Java + Spring Boot's mature ecosystem for PCI compliance, encryption libraries, and audit logging is decisive.
rows.decision: acme-payment is implemented in Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.2. We use the Bouncy Castle JCA provider for FIPS-compliant crypto and Spring's built-in audit event framework for PCI-DSS Requirement 10 logging.

positive: Reduced PCI scope (12 services vs 1 service)
negative: Higher memory footprint per pod (512MB vs 128MB)
Slower CI build times

alternatives.name: Go + FIPS module
alternatives.reason_rejected: Less mature PCI-DSS tooling in Go ecosystem

alternatives.name: Node.js
alternatives.reason_rejected: Insufficient crypto library FIPS validation

rows.title: ADR-006: Python + FastAPI for inventory
rows.context: Inventory data scientists have authored ML-based demand forecasting models in Python. The serving layer must run the same models without re-implementation.
rows.decision: acme-inventory is implemented in Python 3.13 with FastAPI. Models are loaded once at startup and served via async handlers. We use Pydantic v2 for input validation and OpenTelemetry instrumentation throughout.

positive: Single language across data science and serving
negative: Higher cold-start latency on serverless deployments
GIL contention under load

alternatives.name: Export models to ONNX + Go
alternatives.reason_rejected: Forecasting accuracy degrades 4% on edge cases

rows.title: ADR-007: Go for catalog service
rows.context: The catalog service handles 40k requests/sec at peak with a read-heavy access pattern. Performance and low memory footprint are decisive.
rows.decision: acme-catalog is implemented in Go using Gin and a read replica routing layer. Redis cache sits in front with 5-second TTL for hot SKUs.

positive: 30% lower cloud spend vs Node baseline
negative: Team split across Go and Node runbooks

alternatives.name: Rust
alternatives.reason_rejected: Compile time friction outweighs marginal latency gain

rows.title: ADR-008: Elasticsearch for search
rows.context: Full-text + faceted search across 2M SKUs requires sub-100ms p95 query latency. Elasticsearch's BM25 + vector hybrid search has the strongest production track record.
rows.decision: acme-search uses Elasticsearch 8.x with a Node.js query orchestrator. Vector embeddings are generated by a sibling Python service and indexed nightly via a Kafka pipeline.

positive: Hybrid lexical+vector relevance out of the box
negative: Additional infrastructure footprint
Index management overhead

alternatives.name: Postgres pg_trgm + tsvector
alternatives.reason_rejected: Insufficient at 2M SKUs; no native vector search

alternatives.name: Algolia
alternatives.reason_rejected: Per-query pricing exceeds $400k/yr at our scale

rows.title: ADR-009: Python + FastAPI for recommendations
rows.context: Recommendation models (collaborative filtering, content-based, LLM-augmented rerank) are owned by the data science team. The serving layer must match their Python tooling.
rows.decision: acme-recommendations is implemented in Python + FastAPI. Models are versioned in MLflow and loaded lazily. GPU inference is used only for the LLM rerank stage.

positive: Data scientists ship directly without rewrite
A/B test new models in hours not weeks
negative: GPU cost is the largest line item at $28k/month

alternatives.name: TensorFlow Serving
alternatives.reason_rejected: Brittle deployment story; lacks FastAPI flexibility

rows.title: ADR-010: Java for fulfillment orchestration
rows.context: Fulfillment coordinates 5+ third-party logistics APIs (FedEx, UPS, DHL, USPS, OnTrac). Long-running saga patterns and idempotent retry are core requirements.
rows.decision: acme-fulfillment is implemented in Java + Spring Boot using the Temporal workflow engine for saga orchestration. Each carrier integration is a dedicated bounded context with its own worker.

negative: Temporal cluster is additional operational burden

alternatives.name: Camunda
alternatives.reason_rejected: Less idiomatic for Java + weaker observability

alternatives.name: Custom state machine
alternatives.reason_rejected: Reinventing Temporal poorly

rows.title: ADR-011: Go for shipping rate service
rows.context: Shipping rate quotes require sub-200ms p99 latency at checkout. The legacy SOAP integrations with carriers are replaced by gRPC rate-limiting wrappers.
rows.decision: acme-shipping is implemented in Go using gRPC for internal calls and REST for the public checkout integration. Rate-limit budget per carrier is enforced via a token bucket implementation.

alternatives.name: Continue Node.js
alternatives.reason_rejected: Latency floor unacceptable for checkout funnel

rows.title: ADR-012: Node.js for notifications
rows.context: Notifications span email (SES), SMS (Twilio), push (FCM/APNS), and in-app. SDK availability and team familiarity point to Node.js.
rows.decision: acme-notifications is implemented in Node.js + TypeScript. We use BullMQ for retry/backoff and Handlebars for templating.

positive: All four channel SDKs are JS-first
Familiar runtime for the platform team

alternatives.name: AWS Step Functions
alternatives.reason_rejected: Less flexible than BullMQ for in-app channels

rows.title: ADR-013: OpenTelemetry as observability standard
rows.context: 12 services generating telemetry must be aggregated and queryable across the platform. Vendor lock-in (Datadog-only or New Relic-only) is a strategic risk.
rows.decision: All services MUST emit OpenTelemetry traces, metrics, and logs. The OTLP collector is operated in-cluster; vendors (Datadog, Honeycomb, Grafana Cloud) are configurable via a single collector pipeline.

negative: Higher upfront instrumentation work

alternatives.name: Datadog-only
alternatives.reason_rejected: Vendor lock-in; pricing opaque at scale

rows.title: ADR-014: PCI-DSS scope isolation
rows.context: Card data must never touch a non-PCI system. The current implementation has the entire checkout pipeline in PCI scope, multiplying audit cost.
rows.decision: acme-payment is the ONLY system that handles raw PAN data. All other services interact with tokenized references only. The tokenization boundary is enforced by the gateway (Envoy filter + iptables egress rules).

negative: Strict egress rules add deployment friction

alternatives.name: Outsource to Stripe entirely
alternatives.reason_rejected: Loses data ownership; fees compound at $50M ARR scale

rows.title: ADR-015: Blue/green deployment
rows.context: Black Friday requires zero-downtime deployment across all 12 services. The legacy rolling update strategy cannot deliver sub-second cutover.
rows.decision: All production deployments use blue/green via Argo Rollouts. The new revision receives 0% traffic initially, health checks run for 5 minutes, then traffic ramps 10%/min to 100%. Automatic rollback on any SLO breach.

negative: 2x resource consumption during rollout

alternatives.name: Canary
alternatives.reason_rejected: Sub-second cutover not achievable with canary

rows.title: ADR-016: GDPR-compliant data residency
rows.context: EU customers must have their data stored in EU regions. Cross-region replication of customer PII to US is forbidden without explicit consent.
rows.decision: EU customer data is stored exclusively in eu-west-1. A region-aware routing layer ensures EU customers land in EU pods. US customers land in us-west-2. No PII replication across regions.

negative: Operational complexity of dual-region ops

alternatives.name: Single-region (US) with consent
alternatives.reason_rejected: Conversion drops 18% with banner per legal review

rows.title: ADR-017: API versioning strategy
rows.context: Public-facing APIs must support mobile app versions that cannot be force-updated. Breaking changes require a deprecation window.
rows.decision: All public APIs use URL-path versioning (/v1/, /v2/). Deprecated versions receive a minimum 6-month deprecation window with a Sunset header. Mobile clients must support the latest 2 major versions.

positive: Mobile clients can deprecate at their own pace
negative: Dual-version maintenance burden

alternatives.name: Header versioning
alternatives.reason_rejected: Harder to debug; not cache-friendly

rows.title: ADR-018: Governance: Steward + human approval gates
rows.context: Forge's Rule 3 mandates human approval at Architecture, Security, and Deployment boundaries. Acme's earlier missteps (auto-promoted security policy, auto-deployed breaking change) caused customer-visible incidents.
rows.decision: All ADR promotion, security policy changes, and production deployments require explicit human approval. The Steward role is the only role with reset scope=all. Approval workflows are surfaced in the Forge UI as in-context badges.

negative: Slower iteration on policy changes

alternatives.name: Auto-approve with rollback safety net
alternatives.reason_rejected: Rollback is expensive for security and architecture
